sentences.text: My name is Sol Wexler, S-O-L W-E-X-L-E-R.
When I was born, my family name was Weichselblatt, W-E-I-C-H-S-E-L-B-L-A-T-T.
I was born-- now it's Russia.
When I lived-- I was born, it was called the Polish Ukraine.
Sure.
It's-- the town was called Bilcze Zlote, B-I-L-Z-E Z-L-O-T-E.
I was born on March 30th-- on March 4th, 1930.
No.
I made myself two years younger when I came to an orphanage in Germany.
That's 1928.
Look it, now I got to make it 60, right?
I'm sorry.
OK, my father was a merchant.
He used to buy fruit and stuff and ship-- ship it to Upper Poland.
He also had a little farm and a garden.
And my mother used to tend to that.
And she was also a seamstress.
OK, there-- there's going to be two names, born name, the name-- the born name was, would you believe it, [?
Munya ?] Weichselblatt.
You want me to spell it again?
He changed it.
He changed his name when he came to the States and met his brother.
And he had a sister and a brother, and they both changed it to Weichselblatt-- to Wexler, I'm sorry.
Yes.
He changed it from [?
Munya ?] to Ira.
Ira Wexler in America.
We observed modern rituals, I would say.
We went to the shul.
I didn't have payos.
I didn't have a-- I didn't put a [INAUDIBLE] on.
Yes.
Saturday was kept as a holiday.
Both.
Both, with Jews and without Jews.
We spoke, there, a number of languages.
Of course, in the house, we spoke Jewish.
With the Polish people, we spoke Polish.
And with the Ukrainians, we spoke Ukrainian.
You may think it's the same thing, but it isn't.
It's-- it's like putting German and English together, between the Ukraine and the Polish.
Yes.
This was a big town, with a very slow Jewish population.
I would say there were about-- less than 40 Jewish families, out of a few thousand Ukrainians in that particular town.
You want me to--
No.
We were dispersed.
It was quite a large town, except for the Jewish population.
Yes, my father had my grandfather, his sister, his brother, and a large family.
I'm not going to go into names.
On holidays, we would get together.
No, not all of them.
My father-- my grandfather, the one that's in that picture there, on holidays, even though it was not such a big walk, he would not travel.
He would stay with us.
Well, believe it or not, we had-- you could call it a-- a semi-shtiebel.
But it was separate.
It was-- it was a synagogue.
And everybody attended.
Me, too.
Separate.
It
Upstairs.
So I don't remember whether, during the week, they had all times.
I know the-- I remember the holidays.
I don't remember whether we-- unless there was like a bar mitzvah or something similar.
I was bar mitzvahed in my home town in a-- in a small room.
[SHAKES HEAD] It was always dangerous to be in that shul.
Stones were flying from all over.
Even--
The good times, you can call only that you didn't get killed.
And the-- I don't know what questions are coming up, so I don't know what to say.
It's a funny thing.
You know, let's talk about devils.
We were afraid to pass by the synagogue, because, at night, they said they-- these are children.
At night, they said that-- that people were coming down there praying there.
Now as far-- as far as school goes, I had non-Jew-- non-Jewish people that used to do our homework together and all that.
Slowly, it diminished to the point where-- I'll tell you later-- that the person that I lived so good with, it was such an-- it turned out completely, it was-- later, there was a law.
I'll talk about that later.
And I remember going-- going to school was an ordeal and coming home was an ordeal.
I used to be the last one in and the first one out.
Because, otherwise, they would grab us.
Yes.
Yes.
That's right.
We were--
In-- in that same town, in somebody's house, [NON-ENGLISH].
We had a full day.
You know something, I don't remember.
But I don't think they got-- I would ever-- no.
There was some.
I'm sorry, [NON-ENGLISH].
There was some.
Not all of them, but they did go.
I remember going home with [INAUDIBLE], and I had a crush on one.
[LAUGHS]
There is no little bit.
I don't know.
I never knew when my mother got up.
I never knew when my mother went to sleep.
Her maiden name was Lea, but she was called [NON-ENGLISH].
Sternschuss.
And where were we?
We were on-- on-- on-- with the school.
Oh, sorry.
I knew there was something.
No she was born where my grandfather was born, in a town called Myszkow.
You want to spell it, I'll spell it for you, M-Y-S-Z-K-O-W.
This is where my grandfather lived, with this son and daughter.
And--
Very close.
Very close.
The reason I remember, very close, because he only came for holidays.
And he always brought nuts.
He had a nut tree.
[LAUGHS]
Walking distance at half an hour to 45 minutes.
I wouldn't call it far.
But sometimes it looked far.
It was a beautiful town, [INAUDIBLE] than Bilcze Zlote, for example.
Yes.
I walked.
If a car came to our town, the whole town was out looking at it.
We had no electricity.
And like I said, I didn't remember.
I don't remember her going to sleep.
The only time I remember is Friday night, she used to do her own baking.
And we had [NON-ENGLISH].
It's like bialys here.
And when I came to the States, I was looking all over for one.
And I found one [LAUGHS] and-- on-- on Delancey Street.
But getting back to my mother.
I don't think there was a person that didn't like her.
I think most of the-- my cousins spent more time in our house than her own house.
And you know, something, they still cooked good.
She-- she was a very hard-- besides this, she was doing-- she-- she was a-- a sewing.
She would get a--
She would get something, a magazine from-- from the States, and she would copy it, exactly.
Not only this, I'm bringing this up.
Let me describe the town a little bit, so you have a better feel.
We lived in a valley.
And all you saw was trees.
And the lake was the front of the house.
And our-- our house, not the house, the garden was behind the trees.
Very-- very picturesque.
And going back to my mother, again, the neighbors were very close.
I'm talking about non-Jewish neighbors now.
There was no dispute or anything that anybody had, from the Gentile people, that wouldn't come to my mother to find a solution.
And it always helped.
The reason I brought it up, to tell you later how these people turned.
Yes.
His name was Leo.
And he was your younger brother?
He was five years younger than I was.
In other words, he was killed when he was about 10 years old.
He was going only to Hebrew school.
We started at age of seven, seven days a week, at-- besides chaida.
It was--
I didn't get to that part yet.
Don't forget, I'm still with the-- with the Polish Ukrainian people.
And I'm, at that time, about 10 years old.
Now I'll skip to the next chapter.
No.
But in 1939, as you know, Germany invaded Poland.
And nobody thought of the Russians.
The Russians came from my side.
We were on top of the Russians.
Then they started forcing us to write on our Shabbos.
If you had something to do-- all the homework was done on Shabbos.
All the tests were done on the Shabbos.
It came to a point that you couldn't-- if you wanted to go to school, you couldn't exist.
There were a few that were out.
And they got penalties.
There was no such a thing.
You can't-- you can't do it.
The only thing that helped us, a that time, is they were not anti-Jewish, the-- the-- the Russians that came in.
What happened was things got bad, and my father wrote to his brother, whom we never saw in his life, that maybe he can do something to bring him over.
It was a long time-- way-- way before my-- my father was born.
And I remember my mother asking him, maybe we can go together?
And my father said, look, when my brother left, I was a small kid.
I don't know his character.
And I don't know, if I'll call him and ask for you, he made decide to drop the whole project.
I'll go, establish something, and then you will come.
And after three months, that's what happened.
We had papers to go through Poland.
Then we had papers to go through Russia.
And both times, the-- the war intervened.
And unfortunately, my mother was one-- my brother was one of the victims.
It was the most emotional scene, one of the most emotional scenes.
See, we didn't have-- we didn't have horses and wagons.
And my friend's-- my friend's-- my father's friend came.
And he took my father.
And he took me, too.
And we were going from-- from neighbor to neighbor saying goodbye.
When it came-- when it came to go, my brother was sleeping.
And he always brought this up, why didn't you wake me when my father left?
That was his peeve, why didn't you wake me?
Anyways, for me, it was very emotional.
I was crying my-- I don't remember crying that much.
You got on the bus, and I was just holding on.
And when I came home, I knew how it felt losing somebody.
[SOB] And it was very difficult on me.
As a matter of fact, I was difficult to my mother, too.
I don't know.
That I blamed him.
I didn't blame him.
I felt I need somebody.
[SIGHS] After-- after a while, we had different problems to think about.
Oh, absolutely.
You see what happened, we were already-- we didn't know about the Russians coming.
And we decided to run to Romania, which was not too far away.
And while my mother was putting some money into the corset, the things started coming.
And I was scared of that-- those, that I go down into the basement.
Because I remembered that my-- that the Russians, they were called at that time, they were antisemitic at the time, no?
And they came to my grandfather.
They wanted to take his horse away.
I don't know whether he disputed it or not, but they killed him.
They killed my grandfather, my mother's mother.
That was-- I'm named after him, so that was 1917, '18, somewhere around there.
[SIGHS]
I still went to school.
I still--
During-- during the Russian occupation, we didn't have good food.
I remember my mother's two sisters were a little bit well-off to do, and they gave us a cow And food for the cow.
So we had at least milk and cheese, not too much rationed, but we had it.
From-- from his voyage, yes.
From the beginning, yeah, we got mail.
We got a mail before my father reached Warsaw.
May-- maybe you check out and maybe the whole family came out.
And you can imagine my father's feeling.
He was afraid to ask him.
And here, this letter comes.
From his-- from his brother, to try to do whatever we can.
It was too late.
We get papers, three times to go get papers with the Polish government, papers with the Russian government.
They even tried through the German government.
It didn't work.
I might as well mention, three months after he went-- well, Poland was still-- 1938 or '39.
We had papers to go through Japan, to go there.
Yeah.
No, my father went.
My father was in the States, already.
Through-- through Japan to the United States.
Well, then the war broke out with-- it didn't go-- it went against me, whatever we did.
And I took a lot of it out on my mother.
That bothers me absolutely today that I behaved like this.
I was 10 years old.
I had to-- I'm explaining to-- to myself.
I was so frustrated that I had to have an outlet.
I was blaming my mother.
I was blaming my-- my father.
They didn't try hard enough.
It-- it was bad for that time.
In some aspects, worse, in some aspects--
It was hard.
It was harder to get less food.
I'll give you an example.
Even though I had to go Saturday to school, that was improved.
And I guess I might as well-- well mention this, too.
Not too many people know it.
When I was going to Polish school, if you didn't do your homework, you got a punishment.
They could put you-- roll up your pants, put you in the corner, with your hands up, for an hour.
And every week, they accumulated bad points.
And you-- and you had to come and let your pants down.
Yeah, that's what I'm talking about.
Sure.
And they always, the teacher, this-- this Jew knows everything better than you Ukrainians.
You should improve.
I spoke Ukrainian as fluent as any language.
That was the bad part.
The good part is the kids started not bothering us that much.
Because you had, at the [INAUDIBLE], you had a place to go and complain.
Not that it would do any good.
The main language was Russian.
So now-- now I remember Russian better than Polish.
I don't know why.
Up to the age of five, I only spoke Polish.
I didn't speak Jewish.
It was the thing that--
No.
No.
It was Yiddish.
But if you went outside, with your parents, with your friends, so forth, you--
Did they read newspapers?
We got, every month, a package, like this, here, from my aunt.
And that was the newspaper.
In Yiddish.
And there-- there-- there are some very good articles there.
There were romances that were going for months.
God forbid, a paper should get lost.
Another thing I didn't mention, we had no-- did I mention, we had no electricity?
I used to steal a lamp and climb up in the attic and read.
I loved to read.
And it was then, just-- you know, our house was an old-fashioned house, with bricks, but with a straw roof.
And there was-- I mean from the movies, from the other movies you'll see.
There was no-- no way you could go.
You couldn't go.
You go to Russia.
You couldn't go from Russia to Poland.
It was just-- just-- My father, I think, was trying, yes, before where we had letters from our father, every single week.
Every single week, we had letters.
I imagine it was much harder for-- you know, with kids, they play this, and they forget.
But my mother, I don't remember her ever smiling from that point, from there on.
She was-- as a matter of fact, I have all the letters.
My father saved every letter from my mother.
And I found out a lot of things.
From one letter, she mentions you-- you're OK.
You have your family there.
You have your aunt.
It was your aunt's fault.
Your-- your aunt-- your sister, rather, should have tried a little bit harder.
I know my-- the uncle was busy with business.
But you should have tried.
She should have tried.
Letters like this, so I got the feeling how mother felt.
It's a-- it was a very emotional, hard time for me.
The Russians were there for two years.
So me speaking about food, they had the habit, if they've brought something, it lasted for months.
Once, they brought a truck load of halvah.
And we were eating that halvah for breakfast and for lunch and bread and anything.
One time, they brought a whole trainload with spoons.
I don't know what they used them for.
Well, he says, we can't keep them here.
They don't belong.
So he says to the-- it was a captain.
He says to the guy, I'm Jewish.
He had nothing to say anymore.
No.
The only thing we had was the chaida.
And as far as punishment goes, the chaida was worse than the--
No.
No.
Well, if you saw the pictures there, for weddings, it was built like this.
Not-- the-- on holidays, the shul was closed.
And Saturdays, it was open, almost all the time.
During the week, it was closed.
No.
No.
The town was too small.
